query: SDK complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: Beware the outdated SDK: A cautionary tale of broken websites
results.content:
## What happens when an SDK is out of date?

It can cause several problems, including security vulnerabilities and decreased website performance.

Outdated SDKs expose websites to attack by hackers or malicious software, resulting in data breaches, lost revenue, and reputational damage. From a website performance perspective, an outdated SDK can cause compatibility issues with other software and hardware components, leading to system crashes and errors.

This can lead to frustrated users and lost revenue as customers abandon your website for faster, more responsive competitors. Additionally, outdated SDKs may not be compatible with the latest browser versions, resulting in website incompatibility and decreased search engine rankings. [...] ## So, what exactly is an outdated SDK?

SDKs are software development tools for building applications. Developers use these tools to create software that works seamlessly with the platform, including libraries, APIs, and documentation.

However, when an SDK becomes outdated, the tools are no longer current or compatible with the latest platform update. This results in issues like decreased functionality, security vulnerabilities, and even crashes. Therefore, it's crucial to keep your SDKs up-to-date.

In my case, the application uses Directus, which has an SDK called directus/sdk-js. It’s used to get content from the site and requires permissions. Unfortunately, this npm package is deprecated, and completely unsupported which broke the functionality of the application. [...] Additionally, it's important to have a proactive approach to software development and maintenance. This means staying on top of your software inventory, identifying potential risks, and developing a plan to mitigate them before they become critical issues.

In conclusion, using an outdated SDK will have implications for your website's performance, security, and user experience. Keep yourself from falling victim by staying up-to-date, testing your applications after each update, and taking a proactive approach to software development.

Beware the outdated SDK: A cautionary tale of broken websites
Beware the outdated SDK: A cautionary tale of broken websites

results.title: SDK design best practices
results.content:
This comment from Stack Overflow illustrates a developer’s frustrating experience with trying to install the .NET SDK with Visual Studio.

Stack Overflow comment

To prevent this with your SDK, you should address common pain points developers face during installation and setup.

These typically include:

One of the most effective ways to simplify the installation process is to leverage package managers.

These tools automatically handle dependencies, significantly reducing installation complexity.

Package managers can be standalone tools like npm (Node Package Manager) or integrated into popular build tools such as Gradle for Android and Swift for iOS.

Swift dashboard [...] ## Maintain backward compatibility

When updates to an SDK break existing API implementations, developers face unnecessary rework and frustration.

By maintaining backward compatibility, you enable developers to seamlessly adopt new SDK versions without disrupting their workflows.

A widely adopted strategy to achieve this is semantic versioning (SemVer), which uses a three-part versioning scheme to clearly communicate the scope and impact of changes:

Here’s an excerpt from Stripe’s SDK versioning policy:

Stripe sdk versions

To illustrate, imagine your app integrates with a payment API using Stripe SDK version 4.3.2.

If Stripe releases 4.3.3, it would include a bug fix (patch) that requires no code changes to your integration. [...] Conversely, a poorly designed one can push developers away.

The easier your SDK is to use, the more likely it will become a go-to tool in their tech stack, whether they are development agencies or freelancers.

On that note, let’s explore seven best practices for building intuitive, efficient, and developer-friendly SDKs.

Table of Contents

## Prioritize simplicity in setup and installation

To use an SDK, developers must first install it and then configure it for their specific development environment.

This is often the most crucial interaction developers have with your SDK, and that’s why it’s critical to make it effortless.

If the installation is complicated or unclear, it can lead to frustration, and developers might abandon the integration altogether.

results.title: Meta XR SDK broken between 74 - 77 | Meta Community Forums - 1338580
results.content:
## Forum Discussion

##### 🚨 This forum is archived and read-only. To submit a forum post, please visit our new Developer Forum. 🚨

neonoodle's avatar

neonoodle

Explorer

10 months ago

# Meta XR SDK broken between 74 - 77

It looks like the Unity play mode linking to headset is broken as of SDK 74 almost 6 months ago. I've been trying to get a new project going with all of the latest versioning - SDK 77, Unity 6000.1.13f1 and everything I've been doing is by the book from the latest documentation

I just have the camera block and interaction grab block and when I press play the screen on my oculus goes black for 2 seconds and then back to the Link environment. I've tried Unity 6000.0 and 2022 as well and same issue. [...] When I do a build it builds and runs fine on the headset, but I can't preview anything with the play button

After trying all of the different Unity versions, I found this thread on the Unity forum which narrowed it down to headset functionality breaking at 74, so I installed 72 and lo and behold the headset connects fine - passthrough and everything... but the interactions don't work. Hand tracking and controller tracking are broken both in editor and when I build, so using 72 is a non-starter if I can't interact with anything. [...] ##### Other Meta Support

# Your Copilot SDK Sessions Are Broken — Fix Them With This Deep Dive
## Shweta Lodha
14000 subscribers
10 likes [...] 2 comments
### Transcript: [...] reaches 80%, then you start compacting. So, as soon as it reaches 80, it will start that process in behind the scenes and everything will be done in the background. Then you have the buffer exhaustion threshold, which is 95%. So, as soon as our limit reaches 95%, it will basically block the conversation. Maybe it will halt or it will pause for a second until the compaction is done because like I said, this is the background compaction is the background process and there are very high chances that you are still chatting with the agent when compaction is going on. So, in that case it is very important to set up this particular threshold limit so that at 95% everything will be hard stop and things will be paused. So, rather than losing the context and all those, we can go with these

results.title: Building Better User Experience for SDK Products — Three Lessons Learned | by Long Pan | Medium
results.content:
I looked into our developer support emails for certain period. The least popular development environment we support drew the most support calls or emails. On the other hand, our most popular platform, which is iOS, has the least support issues. There are many reasons. For one, our iOS SDK has been tinkered for years and many issues have been ironed out. But obviously as our SDK expands into new programming environments, new platforms, the gains are getting smaller while the effort is piling up, and inevitably, the quality is compromised.

This is not to say that you absolutely don’t want to expand. But you do need to be aware of the cost, and try not to underestimate the effort. A full circle is better than ten broken paths.

A full circle is better than ten broken paths
